text: The surgeon performs limited adhesiolysis, lysing adhesions between the omentum and the sidewall. The colon is mobilized with the peritoneum en bloc, followed by reparietalization. A preperitoneal dissection is initiated, revealing a healthy preperitoneal fat pad that facilitates dissection to the sidewall. The transversus abdominis muscle and underlying transversalis fascia are visible, and the surgeon begins reducing the hernia sac after developing lateral spaces.

The surgeon removes adhesions and identifies the pseudosac, pushing it away from the hernia sac. Circumferential dissection is performed around the hernia neck, followed by mobilization of the kidney and removal of adhesions to the retroperitoneal fat. The dissection extends beyond the defect margins for broad mesh coverage, including subdiaphragmatic dissection near the twelfth rib, with diaphragmatic fibers reflected away from the peritoneum.

The surgeon continues the dissection to develop the lateral space for broad mesh coverage, revealing the retroperitoneal muscular anatomy. Visible structures include the twelfth rib, quadratus lumborum, psoas muscle, and transversus abdominis. Adhesions from the prior operation are carefully taken down, and various nerves such as the subcostal, iliohypogastric, and ilioinguinal nerves are highlighted.

The surgeon prepares for defect closure after thorough dissection, using a number one non-absorbable suture to re-approximate the edges of the lumbar hernia in a transverse orientation. The insufflation pressure is set at twelve, facilitating easy closure with sequential tightening and traction. The lumbar region shows a full thickness injury through the fascia, while central areas have a partial thickness hernia. The surgeon positions heavyweight mesh using a twelve millimeter laparoscopic trocar for easier placement, covering the area to prevent incisional hernia.

The surgeon has placed the mesh over the hernia site, with the kidney reflected throughout the procedure. A standard flap closure is performed using a 2-0 suture on a GS-22 needle, which is larger than usual but effective for closing the robust flap tissue. The trocar is opened to reduce insufflation within the preperitoneal space.
